_doc: Cross-cutting concepts the book repeatedly invokes. `definedIn` points to the note where the concept gets its primary development. `coveredBy` lists chapters that discuss it. Aliases will drive an auto-alias matcher in a later phase.

concepts.label: Receiver budget
concepts.summary: A human has finite time, effective mental hours, and capacity to absorb new information. The constraint is real but not fixed — education, vocabulary, and accumulated engagement expand it.

concepts.label: Complexity ceiling
concepts.summary: The maximum complexity an idea can carry across a network is bounded by network size and accumulated selection/transport pressure. Bigger network, lower ceiling.

concepts.label: Compressed form
concepts.summary: Low-precondition version of an idea. Cheap for receivers to process, plugs into existing mental structures, easy to re-transmit. Free to share, easy to weaponize.

concepts.label: Complex form
concepts.summary: High-precondition version of an idea. Requires receivers to load preconditions, work through implications, decide whether the new structure fits. Expensive but lossless.

concepts.label: Manipulation surface
concepts.summary: What attackers can exploit. Grows with network size in the pure-transport view; with tunable selection-gate criteria in the full view. Recommendation algorithms are selection engines with adjustable weights — the more dangerous formulation.

concepts.label: Three realities (Harari)
concepts.summary: Objective / subjective / intersubjective. The pipeline operates differently on each. Being explicit about which kind of reality is in transit matters for the rest of the book.

concepts.label: Bridge zone
concepts.summary: The space between deep specialists and mass audiences. Where journalism, popularizers, influencers, and AI summarizers live. Where selection pressures are most intense and active reshaping happens.

concepts.label: Echo chambers vs. bubbles (Nguyen)
concepts.summary: Epistemic bubbles are missing-info structures; echo chambers actively discredit outside info. Small networks tend toward chambers, which changes what 'infrastructure for integration' has to do.

concepts.label: Curation layer
concepts.summary: Infrastructure that decouples access from quality assurance — low barriers to enter, robust mechanisms to surface the best work. Wikipedia's editorial apparatus, Stack Overflow reputation. From the democratization-paradox post.

concepts.label: Cost-shifting from producers to consumers
concepts.summary: When barriers to creation fall, the cost of quality assessment doesn't disappear — it transfers from producers to consumers, reviewers, and maintainers who have no more time than before.

concepts.label: Inverted-U relationship
concepts.summary: Refines the binary 'small vs. large networks' framing: there's an optimum. Small networks have their own internal compression dynamics; very large networks collapse under selection pressure.

concepts.label: Creation cheap / review expensive
concepts.summary: When content generation scales faster than curation infrastructure, noise wins regardless of how many gems are in the flood. LLMs accelerate the asymmetry on both sides.

concepts.label: Preservation vs. training (paired institutional functions)
concepts.summary: What 'bureaucracy' was bundling: preservation keeps the complex form alive somewhere; training expands receiver budgets so people can engage with it. Different infrastructure for each.

concepts.label: Memetic fitness
concepts.summary: The local fitness criteria at each selection gate: emotional valence, identity reinforcement, status, action affordance, novelty, controversy. None coordinated, none particularly interested in truth-preservation.

concepts.label: Transferable preconditions
concepts.summary: Field-general capacities (statistical literacy, critical reading, the disposition to look for evidence) that help receivers engage with many domains. The outline's first-pass answer for what makes a bridge node; refined in bridge-nodes-and-versatile-expertise into the paired skill alongside depth rather than the bridge itself.

concepts.label: Versatile expertise
concepts.summary: Deep specialist expertise paired with metacognitive flexibility — the second-order capacity to recognize structural analogies across domains, suspend one's primary paradigm, and treat one's own frame as one option rather than the substrate of reality. The bridge-node mechanism: depth is what gets a bridge admitted as a peer on either side; flexibility is what lets it see and translate the structural analogy that crosses. Refines the outline's 'transferable preconditions' answer into the paired-skill form.

concepts.label: The curse of expertise
concepts.summary: Four cognitive failure modes of deep specialization without paired metacognitive flexibility: paradigm lock-in (can't entertain other frames), overconfidence in cross-application (over-extends mastery), perceptual filtering (screens out information that doesn't fit), conceptual rigidity (can't reframe a problem). Together these are the cognitive substrate of polarization-via-distrust — same mechanism, vocabulary at the individual level instead of the network level.

concepts.label: Handle-ability
concepts.summary: Proposed alternative to 'complexity' as the real gating variable: how many existing mental models an idea plugs into, how much emotional or identity content it carries, whether it tells the receiver what to do. Selection-side property.

concepts.label: Meme ecosystem competition
concepts.summary: Memes don't propagate in isolation — a meme's infection and decay rates are functions of the other memes already competing for the same attention. Fitness is relative to the current population, not an intrinsic property. (Catalog gap flagged by the epidemiological-virality extraction.)

concepts.label: Neutral drift baseline (Yule process)
concepts.summary: The null model for spread: what propagation looks like with no selection at all — random copying plus rich-get-richer accumulation. Most meme variants follow it. Selection is what you detect as departure from this baseline. (Catalog gap flagged by the info-evolution extraction.)

concepts.label: Replicator ontology
concepts.summary: The assumption that culture comes in discrete, copyable units (memes as replicators, analogous to genes). The load-bearing premise of memetics — and the one its critics attack hardest, arguing cultural transmission is reconstructive, not copy-based. (Catalog gap flagged by the memetics-critique extraction.)

concepts.label: Paradox of virality
concepts.summary: What spreads is not what people endorse. Across the political spectrum, people report they do not want high-arousal negative content to go viral — yet it does. The gap implicates the selection gate (especially algorithmic amplification), not receiver preference. (Catalog gap flagged by the psychology-of-virality extraction.)

concepts.label: Superspreader dynamics
concepts.summary: A small number of high-degree nodes drive a disproportionate share of total spread — a network-structure effect with no offline equivalent at the same scale. Distinct from manipulation surface: it's about who is positioned to amplify, not what can be exploited. (Catalog gap flagged by the psychology-of-virality extraction.)

concepts.label: Algorithm as selection engine
concepts.summary: Recommendation algorithms are not lossy transport — they are selection gates with adjustable weights. They make 'who tunes the gates' a literal, operational question, and turn the selection criteria of a few companies into the selection criteria of everyone. (Catalog gap flagged by the psychology-of-virality extraction.)

concepts.label: Epistemic vigilance (mental immune systems)
concepts.summary: Humans have evolved cognitive defenses that screen incoming information for plausibility and source reliability — 'mental immune systems'. Mercier's challenge to the easy-manipulation narrative: receivers are far less gullible than the simple-meme-wins story implies. (Catalog gap flagged by the psychology-of-virality extraction.)

concepts.label: The three-layer message (frame / outer / inner)
concepts.summary: Hofstadter's anatomy of any message (Gödel, Escher, Bach, Ch VI): the frame message ('I am a message, decode me'), the outer message (the decoding mechanism the receiver must already hold — implicit, carried by the medium), and the inner message (the meaning the sender intended). The book adapts it: each pipeline re-encoding can strip a layer independently, and the outer message is what the book calls preconditions.

concepts.label: Network segmentation
concepts.summary: The fracturing of a network into sub-groups along the seams of its binding myth's interpretive latitude. The segments affirm the same myth while decoding it differently, so the network is superficially unified and structurally fragmented at once. Distinct from echo chambers vs. bubbles: this is the process that produces those structures.

concepts.label: Truth-compression regimes (preservation / inversion / orthogonality)
concepts.summary: The three things compression can do to a claim's truth value, set by the gap between the idea's decoding key and the key the receiver supplies: preservation (keys close — a low-resolution true image), inversion (keys opposed — a true claim decoded into a false belief), and orthogonality (the claim is read as an identity flag, its truth value intact but no longer the operative axis). The selection gate decides which regime a compressed variant lands in.

concepts.label: Option space
concepts.summary: The set of forms an idea can take at a given pipeline stage — the menu selection picks from. Transport generates candidate variants; the medium bounds which of them are viable; selection ranks what remains. Selection never enlarges the option space, it only chooses within it, which is why the medium that writes the menu is the higher-leverage and less visible surface.

concepts.label: Technology vs. medium
concepts.summary: Postman's distinction between a technology — the machine or tool itself — and a medium — the social and intellectual environment that grows up around how the technology is used. The book's 'medium' always means the latter: not the device but the environment of capacities, gate-criteria, and cultivated wants it produces. The same technology can host different media, which guards against blaming the hardware for what is really a pattern of use.

concepts.label: Pseudo-context
concepts.summary: Postman's term for a structure invented to give decontextualized information the appearance of a use it does not really have. The information may be true and accurately measured, but it has been stripped of the context that connected it to action; pseudo-context — the news quiz, trivia, conversation-fodder — papers over the gap. Distinct from manufactured content, which never measured anything: pseudo-context is real information made idle.

concepts.label: The Orwell/Huxley axis
concepts.summary: Postman's framing of two opposite failure modes for an information ecosystem. The Orwell mode: control by inflicted pain — censorship, surveillance, information withheld. The Huxley mode: control by inflicted pleasure — truth not concealed but drowned in a sea of engaging irrelevance. The book's diagnosis sits squarely in the Huxley mode: the modern failure is abundance optimized for engagement, not scarcity enforced by a censor.

concepts.label: False consensus among rational agents
concepts.summary: O'Connor & Weatherall's central result: a community of agents who each update on evidence correctly — perfect Bayesians, no irrationality, no gullibility — can still converge on a false belief. False consensus is a property of network structure and the way evidence propagates, not of individual error. It undercuts any diagnosis that locates the problem solely in receivers being careless or stupid.

concepts.label: Credibility weighting
concepts.summary: The practice — rational and unavoidable — of weighting a piece of evidence by how much the receiver trusts its source. In O'Connor & Weatherall's models this is the core mechanism of social belief: no one can verify everything first-hand, so trust in sources does the work. It is also the seam propaganda exploits — manufacture or buy credibility and the weighting carries false evidence as readily as true.

concepts.label: Conformity
concepts.summary: The adoption of a belief because one's peers hold it, rather than on the evidence for it. Distinct from the content-side criteria of memetic fitness: conformity weights the social fact of agreement itself. In O'Connor & Weatherall's models conformity pressure can lock a community onto a belief — true or false — and insulate it from correcting evidence.

concepts.label: Polarization via distrust
concepts.summary: A self-reinforcing loop formalized by O'Connor & Weatherall: when agents discount evidence from sources they distrust, communities that begin with mild disagreement diverge — each side discounts the other's findings, beliefs pull apart, and the divergence deepens the distrust that drove it. Polarization here needs no irrationality and no misinformation; rational trust-weighting plus an initial difference is enough.

concepts.label: The Zollman effect
concepts.summary: The counterintuitive result (Kevin Zollman, central to O'Connor & Weatherall's account) that increasing a community's connectivity can reduce the accuracy of its beliefs: densely connected agents see each other's preliminary, noisy results too quickly and converge prematurely on whichever answer took an early lead. More communication is not monotonically better — there is an optimum. Bears directly on the Part IV integration question.
